coredata.dc:title: Environmentally benign sensing platform for label free detection of Fe3+ and tobramycin using highly fluorescent carbon dots valorized from sweet potato roasting residues
coredata.dc:creator: Abdella, Aya A.
El-Malla, Samah F.
coredata.dc:description: A novel sustainable source with zero intended energy consumption is introduced for the first time for obtaining carbon dots (CDs). These CDs were spontaneously formed during the ordinary roasting of sweet potato (SPCDs) with a yield of 160.54 mg/kg. The obtained SPCDs exhibited high fluorescence quantum yield (53%) at 442 nm after excitation at 360 nm, narrow particle size distribution (3.54 ± 1.08 nm) and negative zeta potential (-12.2 mV). Using the obtained SPCDs, a sensitive, selective, rapid, and effortless turn off–on fluorescent sensing platform was constructed for label free detection of the non chromophoric drug, tobramycin (TOB). The proposed sensing strategy was based on the possible Fe3+ induced mixed mode static quenching/inner filter effect and fluorescence recovery through its complexation by TOB. The sensor demonstrated good performance for TOB detection with a linear range of 10.69 – 106.90 µM (5–50 µg/mL). The sensing platform was successfully applied for TOB detection in its ophthalmic solution, with good accuracy (100.39% ± 2.19) and precision (RSD, 2.18%), and can be adopted for its routine analysis without any chemical derivatization. Moreover, the developed sensor was successfully applied for TOB detection in real samples including honey and river water. Such sustainable and cost-effective carbon dots require neither special equipment nor hazardous chemicals so that it can be adopted as a source for CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sweet potato
Carbon dots
Turn off–on sensor
Tobramycin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots and sensor solution
Preparation and production yield calculation of SPCDs
Optical properties and characterization of SPCDs
Detection of Fe3+ using the proposed SPCDs sensor
Detection of TOB using the proposed SPCDs sensor
Mechanism investigation of the proposed SPCDs sensor
Comparison with other sweet potato carbon dots.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Dr. Ing. Ahmed Zayed at the institute of bioprocess engineering, Technical University of Kaiserslautern, Germany, for his kind assistance. This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV spectrum of SPCDs sensor solution, excitation, and emission fluorescence spectra of SPCDs solution (Figure inset photos of SPCDs sensor solution under day light (1) and blue light 365 nm (2). (b) Overlay of emission spectra of SPCDs at different λex (300–400 nm) (c) FTIR spectrum of SPCDs showing the major functional groups. (d) Zeta potential measurement for recording the surface charge of the SPCDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM images of SPCDs showing uniform spherical carbon dots (inset particle size distribution histogram), (b) TEM images of the obtained SPCDs after addition of Fe3+ showing aggregated carbon dots, (c) HRTEM images of the SPCDs showing lattice fringe of 0.2 nm. (d) SAED pattern of SPCDs.

(a) The response of SPCDs towards various metal ions at 0.25 mM of each (asterisk (*) indicates significantly different quenching attained by Fe 3+). (b) SPCDs FL quenching upon addition of Fe3+ (200 µM) and FL recovery by 30 µg/ml TOB.

(a) FL quenching of SPCDs using different concentrations of Fe3+ (0 – 250 µM). (b) The linear relationship between FL quenching and Fe3+ concentration within the range of 25–250 µM. (c) FL recovery of SPCDs-Fe3+ with various concentrations of TOB (5–50 µg/mL). (d) The linear relationship between the FL recovery and TOB concentration within a range of 5–50 µg/mL.

The demonstrated selective FL recovery of SPCDs-Fe3+ by TOB in comparison with some possible interferents, gentamicin (GEN), neomycin (NEO), spiramycin (SPI), benzalkonium chloride (BZC), boric acid, sodium sulfate, 30 µM of each, and sodium chloride (0.9%).
Diagrammatic representation of SPCDs preparation procedures and proposed sensing platform for Fe3+ and TOB detection.
Regression parameters for Fe3+ and TOB detection using the proposed SPCDs sensor at optimum conditions.
Recovery of TOB in Tobrin® ophthalmic solution using the proposed sensor and a reference colorimetric method.

head.ce:title: Environmentally benign sensing platform for label free detection of Fe3+ and tobramycin using highly fluorescent carbon dots valorized from sweet potato roasting residues

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, The Medical Campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Medical Campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sustainable source with zero intended energy consumption is introduced for the first time for obtaining carbon dots (CDs). These CDs were spontaneously formed during the ordinary roasting of sweet potato (SPCDs) with a yield of 160.54 mg/kg. The obtained SPCDs exhibited high fluorescence quantum yield (53%) at 442 nm after excitation at 360 nm, narrow particle size distribution (3.54 ± 1.08 nm) and negative zeta potential (-12.2 mV). Using the obtained SPCDs, a sensitive, selective, rapid, and effortless turn off–on fluorescent sensing platform was constructed for label free detection of the non chromophoric drug, tobramycin (TOB). The proposed sensing strategy was based on the possible Fe3+ induced mixed mode static quenching/inner filter effect and fluorescence recovery through its complexation by TOB. The sensor demonstrated good performance for TOB detection with a linear range of 10.69 – 106.90 µM (5–50 µg/mL). The sensing platform was successfully applied for TOB detection in its ophthalmic solution, with good accuracy (100.39% ± 2.19) and precision (RSD, 2.18%), and can be adopted for its routine analysis without any chemical derivatization. Moreover, the developed sensor was successfully applied for TOB detection in real samples including honey and river water. Such sustainable and cost-effective carbon dots require neither special equipment nor hazardous chemicals so that it can be adopted as a source for CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) are promising and attractive materials that have been applied for wide range of biomedical applications including biosensing, drug delivery, tissue engineering, diagnosis, cell imaging, and cancer therapy [1]. Among the different CBNs, carbon dots (CDs) have been recently recognized as a rising star in analysis due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of preparation, as well as stability [2]. Additionally, CDs are excellent fluorescent materials owing to their unique characteristics such as higher quantum yield (QY), more photostability, non-blinking, and resistance to photobleaching compared to traditional fluorescent dyes. Therefore, they have been incorporated in a wide variety of sensing platforms for detection of different non-fluorogenic analytes including heavy metal (e.g., Fe, Ag, and Cu) [3], drugs (e.g., metronidazole, ascorbic acid) [4], and environmental pollutants (e.g., trinitrotoluene) [5].

The global energy crisis prompted researchers to find solutions that could enable them to reduce energy consumption and make use of wastes. Many efforts have been devoted to make use of bio-wastes as precursors for CDs synthesis, allowing the conversion of non-valuable waste to high-value products [2,6]. Biowastes have been applied as sustainable and cost-effective carbon source for producing O, N, and S doped CDs with water compatible surface functionality (–OH, –COOH, –NH, -SH,.…etc) [6]. Consequently, the use of biomass-derived CDs has been explored by analysts to develop eco-friendly, inexpensive, and non-toxic probes [7]. Among the reported biomasses, sweet potato has been widely applied for CDs preparation. Several methods were reported for preparing fluorescent CDs based on sweet potato as a carbon source [8–10]. Most of the reported methods adopted hydrothermal treatment either of sweet potato extract [10], peels [8] or the whole tissues [11,12]. Despite of being considered green and efficient, some economical concerns can be raised about these preparation methods as they waste an edible part of the plant or consumes a large amount of energy (by pyrolysis at very high temperature for long time reaching 18 h [10]). The maximum ecological benefit can be achieved by valorizing non-edible plant parts as carbon sources with minimal energy consumption.

Tobramycin (TOB) is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that is most used for gram positive bacterial infections. Its ophthalmic solution has been used for the treatment of eye infections. Owing to its high polarity, saturated ring system and lack of suitable chromophore, the determination of TOB in different matrices is highly challenging [13]. Few colorimetric [14,15] and spectrofluorimetric methods [16,17] are available in literature for TOB determination in dosage form. These methods suffer from long reaction time and tedious derivatization steps. Additionally, some high performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC) methods have been reported for its detection using either unconventional detectors, such as refractive index [18], electrochemical detection [19] and evaporative light scattering detectors [20], or using ultraviolet (UV) detection with additional pre- or post-column derivatization [21,22], which is also tedious and time consuming to be used for routine analysis. Consequently, it is essential to develop simple, fast, cost-effective, and sustainable procedures for TOB detection.

This work describes highly fluorescent sweet potato derived carbon dots (SPCDs) based sensor that was prepared, for the first time, without any intended energy consumption as the SPCDs were spontaneously formed during the regular roasting of sweet potato. Compared to the reported CDs, this facile preparation required neither special equipment nor hazardous chemicals, achieved high production yield with no intended energy consumption. The fluorescence intensity of the obtained SPCDs could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Fe3+ and consequently was successfully applied for its detection in various water samples. Moreover, based on the affinity of TOB to complex Fe3+, a fast, sustainable, cost-effective turn off–on label free sensing platform was developed for single step detection of TOB (scheme 1
). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report for TOB detection using CBNs.

Sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas, Convolvulaceae; order Solanales) was obtained from local market (Tanta, Egypt), cultivated and autumn harvested by ZIRAIA CO (Alexandria, Egypt). The plant collection and use were in accordance with all the relevant guidelines. TOB (99.99 %) was kindly supplied by Sigma Company for Pharmaceutical Industries (Quesna, Egypt). Anhydrous ferric chloride (laboratory reagent grade) was obtained from Srl chemicals (Mumbai, India). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Alpha Chemika (Mumbai, India). Ferrous sulfate was supplied by Riedel-de Haën (Hannover, Germany). Boric acid and other metal salts (laboratory reagent grade) including CuSO4·5H2O, CoCl3, SnCl2, MgSO4, KCl, NaCl, Na2SO4, NaCH3COO, BaCl2, Pb (CH3COO)2, CaCl2, Cr2(SO4)3·6H2O, ZnCl2, AlCl3, and AgNO3 were obtained from Isochem (https://www.isochemlab.com) (Vert-le-Petit, France). Tobrin®, 0.3% TOB ophthalmic solution produced by Egyptian International Pharmaceutical Industries Co (EPICO) (10th of Ramadan city, Egypt), was purchased from a local community pharmacy. Neomycin, gentamicin, and spiramycin were kindly supplied by Sigma Company for Pharmaceutical Industries (Quesna, Egypt). River water samples were collected from Bahr Shebeen Nilotic Canal (Tanta, Egypt), filtered through a 0.45 µm Millipore filter, and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C until further utilized. Tap water samples were collected from Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry lab, Faculty of pharmacy Tanta University (Tanta, Egypt), and filtered through a 0.45 µm Millipore filter. Honey was purchased from local supermarket.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-6300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with scan speed of 1000 nm/min and slit width adjusted at 5 nm for both excitation and emission using Spectra manager software (version 1.55). The UV spectra were recorded on a Jasco V-530 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). A Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 ATR-FTIR (Utah, USA) was used for identification of surface functional groups of the obtained SPCDs. Zeta potential measurements were conducted on a Malvern Zetasizer (Cambridge, UK) equipped with disposable zeta cell. Elemental analysis was performed using an Elementar Vario Micro cube apparatus (Langenselbold, Germany) using tin combustion panels. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were conducted using a JEOL (JEM- 2100F) High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Tokyo, Japan) operated at accelerated voltage of 200 mV. Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner bath 3510 (Danbury, USA), Memmert water bath (Buechenbach, Germany), HANNA pH211 pH-meter with double junction glass membrane electrode (Woonsocket, USA), and Hettich centrifuge (Tuttlingen, Germany) were utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots and sensor solution
ce:section.ce:para: Sweet potato was processed for roasting as follows: sweet potato was soaked in water, washed to remove residual soil, dried, and placed on a white parchment paper. Then, it was roasted without pricking in a domestic oven at 200 °C for 90 min. The ignited black residues were collected, converted to fine powder by compression between two wax paper sheets, and stored in dry clean container until further utilized (scheme 1 ).

For sensor solution preparation, the SPCDs were extracted by placing 0.1 g of the collected residue in 100 mL distilled water. Then, the solution was sonicated for 30 min, centrifuged (5 min, 6000 rpm), filtered through 0.22 µm syringe filter. The SPCDs solution was stable when stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for one month.

For detection of Fe3+, different volumes from 10 to 50 µL of 5 mM Fe 3+ solution was added to 800 µL SPCDs (0.046 mg/mL) and 700 µL buffer (pH 3.0). The solution was made up to 2 mL using distilled water. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 442 nm after excitation at 360 nm. Calibration curve was constructed by plotting the F/F0 ratio vs the corresponding Fe3+ solution concentration (mM), where F is the FL intensity of the SPCDs-Fe3+ and F0 is the initial FL intensity before Fe3+ addition.

For detection of Fe3+ in real water samples, the collected water samples (distilled, tap, or river water) were spiked to obtain Fe3+ concentrations of 50, 125, 250 µM. Typically, 500 µL of the spiked water samples were analysed using the above-mentioned procedures.

For TOB detection, different volumes from 10 to 60 µL of stock standard TOB (1 mg/mL) were added to 800 µL of SPCDs sensor solution, and 80 µL of 5 mM FeCl3. The solution was made up to 2 mL using distilled water. The FL was recorded at 442 nm after excitation at 360 nm. Calibration curve was constructed by plotting normalized FL intensity (F-F0 /F0) vs the corresponding TOB concentration, where F is the FL intensity of the SPCDs-Fe3+ after addition of TOB and F0 is the initial FL intensity of SPCDs-Fe3+.

For TOB detection in Tobrin® ophthalmic solution, it was diluted with distilled water to obtain TOB concentration of 1 mg/mL. An aliquot of 60 µL of this solution was analysed following the above-mentioned procedure for TOB detection.

For TOB detection in real honey samples, honey samples were processed following the reported procedure [23]. Honey samples were subjected to 10-fold dilution with distilled water. An aliquot of 500 µL of diluted honey solution was analysed following the above-mentioned procedure for TOB detection.

For detection of TOB in real water samples, the collected river water samples were spiked to obtain TOB concentrations of 30 µg/mL. Typically, 1 mL of the spiked water samples were analysed using the above-mentioned procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and production yield calculation of SPCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, we came across the presence of CDs in sweet potato roasting residues when their aqueous extract showed blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1
(a)). Sweet potato was subjected to regular roasting in a domestic oven at 200 °C until for 90 min. Fig. S1 shows picture of representative examples of the used sweet potato before and after roasting in addition to the ignited sugar residues formed during their roasting. These residues are formed mainly from the juices drained during roasting which are rich in carbohydrates [24], as suggested by their hygroscopic nature as well as elemental analysis (49.5% C, 4.81% H, and 0.7 % N). The total mass of the roasting residues was found to be 3.49 g/kg sweet potato. The SPCDs production yield was calculated according to the procedure described in section A, supplementary material, and was found to be 160.54 mg/kg sweet potato. This production yield is considered very high regarding energy consumption, spontaneous formation, and making use of wastes [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and characterization of SPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of SPCDs were investigated using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra. The UV spectrum of the SPCDs sensor solution (Fig. 1 (a)) displays a broad band at 320 nm which corresponds to n-π* of C = O, while C = C π-π* transition was observed at 270 nm. An intense blue coloured emission was observed under UV light (365 nm), which indicates the high fluorescence emission of the investigated SPCDs (Fig. 1 (a), inset photos). SPCDs solution exhibited high blue emission at 442 nm after excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The QY of the formed SPCDs was determined using standard quinine sulfate, according to the procedure described in section B supplementary material, and was found to be 53% [4]. This QY is considered very high for CDs prepared from bio precursors [6]. Furthermore, the obtained SPCDs solution exhibited excitation independent emission (Fig. 1 (b)) indicating a narrow particle size distribution [25].

The different oxygen containing functional groups were evident by the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 (c)). The FTIR spectrum of SPCDs showed broad band between 3300 and 3600 cm−1, intense sharp band at 1702 cm−1, sharp band at 1599 cm−1, and strong band at 1025 cm−1 that can be ascribed to OH, C = O, C = C, and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively. The obtained results suggested the OH, Carbonyl, and Carboxylic functionalization of SPCDs [10]. In order to verify the surface charge of the SPCDs, the zeta potential was measured in aqueous medium and was found to be –12.2 mV, as shown in Fig. 1 (d). The negative potential result designated the existence of a negatively charged functional group on SPCDs surface. These negative functional groups could enhance the solubility, improve stability (by hindering aggregation induced quenching), and increase the nucleophilicity allowing the particles to interact with heavy metals such as Fe3+, Cu2+,….…etc [26]. This was shown in Fig. 2
(b), which demonstrated the aggregation of SPCDs induced upon the addition of Fe3+.

The existence of CDs with an average size of 3.54 ± 1.08 nm was confirmed from the HRTEM micrograph, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). The TEM images clearly revealed that the SPCDs are uniform and spherical in shape with a narrow size distribution range (Fig. 2 (a) inset). This narrow particle size distribution could account for the excitation independent emission observed in Fig. 1 (b), which is a characteristic feature of narrow particle size range CDs [25]. The SPCDs exhibited lattice fringe with a d-spacing of 0.20 nm (Fig. 2 (c)), which corresponds to the (100) plane lattice of graphite [27]. This indicates the crystalline nature of the SPCDs which was confirmed by SAED pattern shown in Fig. 2 (d). The elemental composition of the SPCDs was determined using EDX-TEM ( Fig. S2 ). The atomic composition of C and O were estimated to be 93.22% and 6.78%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ using the proposed SPCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface functionalization of the obtained SPCDS suggested possible interaction with heavy metals as indicated by the negative zeta potential. The reactivity of the SPCDs towards various metal ions was investigated using 0.25 mM of each. As shown in Fig. 3
(a), only Fe3+ could significantly (ANOVA at 95% confidence level) quench the SPCDs FL as indicated by lowest F/ F0, where other metal ions resulted in very low or no quenching. This can be attributed to the pH and buffer type dependent FL quenching exhibited by carboxylate terminated carbon dots in presence of different metals [28]. As reported in literature, the FL intensity of CDs with carboxylic surface functionality are selectively and significantly quenched by Fe3+ in acetate buffer (pH 3.0). This indicated the potential application of the sensor for Fe3+ detection in water samples where the possible coexisting metal ions would not interfere. The conditions were optimized for Fe3+ detection by adjusting pH, volume of buffer, concentration of SPCDs, and time.

Both the FL intensity of SPCDs solution and the degree of quenching induced by Fe3+ addition was affected by pH change. Therefore, the ability of Fe3+ to quench FL of SPCDs was studied over a pH range from 2.0 to 10.0. The obtained results are presented in Fig. S3
(a) and (b), which showed that as the pH increases above 2.0, FL quenching (F/F0) was increased which may be attributed to the ionization of surface –COOH functionality, suggesting its essentiality for Fe3+ sensing. On the other hand, at pH ˃ 5.0, minimal quenching was observed which can be attributed to the poor solubility of Fe3+. The maximum FL quenching was achieved at pH 3.0, as shown in Fig. S3
(b). The added volume of buffer was further investigated, and the optimum volume was found to be 700 µL ( Fig. S3
(c)). The concentration of SPCDs in sensor solution was studied revealing the maximum FL quenching was observed using 0.0175 mg/mL ( Fig. S3
(d)). Furthermore, the addition of Fe3+ to the SPCDs sensor resulted in immediate quenching that was stable for 30 min ( Fig. S4 ), indicating the ability of the proposed sensor to selectively detect Fe3+ with acceptable repeatability.

At optimum conditions, Fe3+ could quantitatively quench SPCDs FL over a concentration range of 25.0–25.0 µM (Fig. 4
(a)) with acceptable linearity (R2 = 0.995), as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The regression parameters for Fe3+ detection using the proposed sensor are presented in Table 1 . The detection limit (DL) was calculated using the formula 3.3 σ/S (where σ is the SD of the intercept and S is the slope of calibration curve) and was found to be 13.96 µM.

The proposed sensing strategy using the SPCDs sensor was successfully applied for Fe3+ detection in spiked distilled water, tap water, and river water samples. As shown in Table S1 , Fe3+ could be detected in spiked water samples with acceptable accuracy and precision, indicated by % recovery and % RSD, respectively. Furthermore, the mean % recovery of Fe3+ from different spiked water samples at each concentration level were statistically analysed using ANOVA (p = 0.05) test to detect any possible interference from matrix. No significant differences were detected between the % recovery calculated for each water type, as indicated by p-value of 0.12. This demonstrated the selectivity of the proposed SPCDs sensor for Fe3+ detection without any interferences from the tested matrices. As compared to available CBNs reported for Fe3+ detection, our proposed sensor has the advantage of being zero-energy consuming, sustainable, facile, mild, and valorising and reducing wastes as well as being cost effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TOB using the proposed SPCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The addition TOB into SPCDs-Fe3+ sensor system resulted in observed turning-on of the quenched fluorescence (Fig. 3 (b)). The effect of pH, Fe3+ concentration, SPCDs concentration in sensor solution, and time were studied to determine the optimum conditions at which max FL recovery could be achieved. The pH of the sensor solution was studied over the range of 2–10 ( Fig. S5
(a)). Highest FL recovery was achieved between pH 5.0 – 7.0, with no differences from these conducted in aqueous solution. Therefore, no buffer was used for TOB sensing. The added concentration of Fe3+ was studied and 200 µM was selected as it could enable TOB detection with high sensitivity and broader concentration range ( Fig. S5
(b)). Smaller Fe3+ concentrations narrowed the response range and decreased the sensitivity of TOB sensing. On the other hand, higher Fe3+ concentrations resulted in over quenching of SPCDs leading to decreased sensitivity and impaired linearity. The TOB induced turning-on was studied at different SPCDs concentration levels. As shown in Fig. S5
(c), The max F/F0 ratio was achieved when the SPCDs concentration in sensor solution was maintained at 0.0184 mg/mL. The smaller SPCDs concentrations would narrow the FL window within which the turn-off–on system works, while higher SPCDs concentrations would interfere with TOB induced FL recovery due to primary inner filter effect, as shown in Fig. S5
(d). The addition of TOB to the SPCDs-Fe3+ sensor resulted in immediate FL recovery that was stable for 30 min ( Fig. S4 ), indicating its potential application to routine analysis of TOB.

At the optimized conditions, the TOB induced FL recovery was found linear (R2 = 0.995) over a concentration range of 5–50 µg/mL (Fig. 4 (c) and (d)). The regression parameters including slope, intercept and residual SD were calculated and presented in Table 1. The DL was calculated using the formula 3.3 σ/S (where σ is the SD of the intercept and S is the slope of calibration curve) and was found to be 3.64 µg/mL. Furthermore, the proposed SPCDs sensor was successfully applied for TOB detection in Tobrin® ophthalmic solution. The accuracy and precision of the sensing strategy were indicated by a mean % recovery of 100.39 ± 2.19 and %RSD of 2.18%, respectively (Table 2 ). Moreover, the selectivity of the proposed SPCDs sensor for TOB detection was investigated by comparing TOB elicited FL recovery with other structurally related aminoglycoside antibiotics (neomycin and gentamicin), a macrolide antibiotic (spiramycin), in addition to some other inactive ingredients labelled to be present in Tobrin®, such as benzalkonium chloride, boric acid, sodium chloride, and sodium sulfate. The proposed SPCDs sensor demonstrated very good selectivity for TOB detection as shown in Fig. 5 . In addition, the % recovery values obtained using the proposed SPCDs sensor were further compared to a reported colorimetric method, where TOB was oxidized by alkaline copper sulfate solution [29]. Both F-test and t-test were applied, and the calculated p-values are presented in Table 2. These results demonstrated that there is no significant difference between the results obtained by both methods, indicating the potential application of the proposed sensor for TOB detection in its ophthalmic solution. The proposed SPCDs sensor has the advantage of being very simple, ecofriendly, sustainable, and cost-effectively prepared. This label free sensor system has enabled the sensitive fast single step detection of TOB in its commercial eye drops without any chemical derivatization, long reactions, or severe conditions that are required in most of the reported methods listed in Table S2 . It is worthy to note that the proposed sensor demonstrated comparable sensitivity to reported chemical derivatization methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism investigation of the proposed SPCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 3 (b), Fe3+ quenched the SPCDs FL intensity which could be quantitively turned on by TOB. FL quenching can occur by one or more of these mechanisms: dynamic quenching, static quenching, or inner filter effect (IFE) [31]. To figure out the possible interaction mechanisms involved in this sensing strategy, TEM image was acquired for SPCDs-Fe3+ and compared with SPCDs before addition of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the TEM image of SPCDs-Fe3+ showed aggregates of CDs formed upon addition of Fe3+. This aggregation could be visually detected as turbidity using high concentrations of Fe3+, indicating the static quenching as a possible mechanism.

Furthermore, the optical properties of SPCDs were utilized for deeper understanding of their interaction mechanism with Fe3+. In static quenching, the formation of ground state complex can result in changing the UV absorption spectrum of the fluorophore [32]. As shown in Fig. S6
(a), it was evident that the UV absorption spectrum of SPCDs-Fe3+ was different from that of the mathematically added SPCDs and Fe3+ spectra. Static quenching was assisted by restoring the quenched FL either by precipitation of Fe3+ using phosphate or complexation using EDTA [33,34]. Furthermore, minimal FL quenching was observed using Fe3+ ammonium sulfate salt (complexed form of Fe3+), as shown in Fig. S6
(b). This assisted the static quenching and suggested the contribution of secondary IFE. As shown in Fig. S6
(c), the spectral overlap between the UV spectrum of Fe3+ and excitation spectrum of SPCDs indicated a minimal contribution of inner filter effect and this can explain why the FL intensity of the SPCDs-Fe3+ could not be totally restored by TOB. Moreover, based on the reported complex formation affinity between TOB and Fe3+ [35], it could dissociate the formed SPCDs-Fe3+ complex and restore the quenched SPCDs FL.

The FL quenching data were fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation (Eq
(1)
[33] by plottingF0/Fversus [Q] graph, whereF0 andFare the FL intensities of SPCDs in the absence and presence of the quencher (Fe3+), respectively, [Q] is the quencher concentration, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. A linear Stern-Volmer plot was obtained ( Fig. S6
(d)) conforming to static quenching [10].
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S
V
Q

Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer constant KSV was determined at different temperatures including, 25, 40, and 50 °C. As illustrated in Fig. S6
(d), the KSV was decreased by increasing the temperature ( Table S4 ), which is a characteristic behaviour of static quenching, as the increase in temperature results in dissociation of SPCDs-Fe3+ complex [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other sweet potato carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a sustainable cost-effective turn off–on spectrofluorimetric CDs-based sensing platform was prepared with zero energy consumption and was established for the selective detection of Fe3+ and TOB. The developed sensor consisted of SPCDs spontaneously formed in the conventional roasting residues of sweet potato in domestic gas oven. The proposed sensing strategy enabled the sensitive, selective, accurate, and precise detection of TOB in its ophthalmic solution and real samples including honey and river water. The constructed sensing platform enabled label free TOB detection without any tedious time-consuming chemical derivatization. This work introduces a novel sustainable supply for cost-effective eco-friendly CDs production with high QY, zero-energy consumption, and high production yield.

Aya A. Abdella: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Samah F. El-Malla: Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Dr. Ing. Ahmed Zayed at the institute of bioprocess engineering, Technical University of Kaiserslautern, Germany, for his kind assistance.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108844.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
